1.blurb: Eridu, the southernmost Sumerian settlement, has the oldest village layers of any city in Mesopotamia — going back to roughly 5400 BCE, older than Susa's earliest 4200 BCE occupation. Mesopotamia Chapter 2 makes Eridu's case as the oldest city in the world, and it's the one Sumerian site that genuinely predates the first Susa village.

The proto-cuneiform tablets from Uruk IV appear around 3200 BCE — essentially the same century as the Proto-Elamite tablets at Susa, and each invented independently. Mesopotamia Chapter 3 tells the Uruk side: temple bureaucrats inventing the world's first writing to track grain rations and beer allocations. Two neighboring civilizations crossing the writing threshold in parallel.

1.matchText: earliest Indus proto-signs at sites like Harappa
1.blurb: The Ravi Phase settlement at Harappa was scratching simple symbols onto pottery around 3300 BCE — roughly the same century that Proto-Elamite tablets appeared at Susa, Sumerian proto-cuneiform at Uruk, and the earliest Egyptian hieroglyphs. Indus Valley Chapter 3 covers the Kot Diji / Ravi Phase origins of what would eventually become the still-undeciphered Indus script.

Shortughai was the permanent Harappan trading outpost in northern Afghanistan, 930 miles from the Indus heartland, founded specifically to control the lapis lazuli and tin sources that Elamite caravans then moved west across the Zagros. Indus Valley Chapter 7 tells the maritime-and-overland story of the Harappan trade network that Elam sat on the overland leg of.

1.matchText: long-drilled etched beads in the Harappan carnelian workshops at Chanhu-daro and Dholavira
1.blurb: The Harappan carnelian bead industry was one of the signature Bronze Age luxury exports — long, heat-treated, acid-etched beads drilled with copper tools over weeks of labor per batch, shipped west through Elam to Mesopotamian royal tombs. Indus Valley Chapter 7 covers the workshops and the bead factories that supplied the Near East.

1.matchText: Queen Puabi's headdress and the other Royal Tombs of Ur
1.blurb: Queen Puabi's death pit at Ur contained 74 sacrificed attendants, gold jewelry, and the most famous lapis-and-carnelian headdress of the Bronze Age — all of it routed to Ur through Elamite overland caravans and Indus maritime trade. Mesopotamia Chapter 4 walks through the Royal Tombs and the wealth they document.

2.matchText: Naram-Sin had himself deified while still alive
2.blurb: Naram-Sin's self-deification was the first Mesopotamian claim of living divinity, but his successor-once-removed Shulgi of Ur III pushed the move even further: temples to himself as a living god, hymns composed in his divine honor, and a propaganda boast that he could run from Ur to Nippur and back in a single day. Mesopotamia Chapter 6 covers Shulgi's god-king experiment.

3.blurb: The Amorites were Semitic-speaking migrants from the Syrian desert who flooded into the vacated Sumerian plain after the Elamite sack of Ur. Within a generation they had founded small kingdoms across Mesopotamia, one of which would become Babylon. Mesopotamia Chapter 6 tells the story of the post-Ur III transition that ended Sumerian as a living language.

3.matchText: oldest surviving example of a specific literary genre called the
3.blurb: The Lament for Ur is the prototype city-lament — a Sumerian literary form that would be imitated for centuries afterward. Mesopotamia Chapter 6 treats it as both a historical source and one of the great grief documents of the ancient Near East. No other Bronze Age civilization produced anything like it; the urban collapses of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa passed in archaeological silence.

The Shang oracle bones from Anyang record repeated brother-to-brother throne transfers rather than father-to-son inheritance — exactly the pattern the Shimashki and Sukkalmah Elamite dynasties would formalize. Ancient China Chapter 3 covers the Shang succession system, which is the closest structural parallel anywhere in the ancient world to Elam's collective kingship.

4.blurb: Hammurabi is remembered today as the master strategist who unified Babylonia, but in his first fifteen years he was an Elamite client who addressed Siwe-palar-hupak as his diplomatic 'father.' Mesopotamia Chapter 7 covers his rise and the Code of Hammurabi — the same stele Shutruk-Nahhunte would later drag to Susa as war loot.

The Mari letters are the richest diplomatic archive of the early second millennium BCE and cover Hammurabi's rise to power from the Mesopotamian side — along with the fact Babylon's later chroniclers deliberately suppressed, that Hammurabi was an Elamite client king for the first fifteen years of his reign. Mesopotamia Chapter 7 walks through his patient rise and the eventual break with Siwe-palar-hupak.

5.matchText: Ramesses II was carving the colossal rock-cut temples at Abu Simbel
5.blurb: While Untash-Napirisha was finishing Chogha Zanbil around 1250 BCE, Ramesses II was carving four 66-foot-tall seated colossi of himself into the cliff face at Abu Simbel, deep in Egyptian-occupied Nubia. Ancient Nubia Chapter 7 covers New Kingdom Egypt's Nubian province, the Viceroy of Kush, and the monument-building competition that ran in parallel with Elam's Middle Elamite peak.

5.matchText: Shang dynasty bronze tradition of central China
5.blurb: Within a century of Napir-Asu's nearly-two-ton solid-bronze statue at Susa, Shang bronze workers at Anyang were casting the Houmuwu Ding — a 1,832-pound rectangular ritual cauldron — using piece-mold techniques completely different from the Elamite approach. Ancient China Chapter 3 covers the Shang bronze tradition. Two unrelated civilizations solving the same monumental-bronze problem at the same moment, at opposite ends of Asia.

5.matchText: one of the three or four great powers of the Late Bronze Age Near East
5.blurb: Middle Elamite Elam was part of the Late Bronze Age great-power club documented in the Amarna Letters — the cuneiform diplomatic archive in which Kassite Babylonian, Mitanni, Hittite, and Assyrian kings all wrote to Egypt as peers. Mesopotamia Chapter 8 covers the diplomatic system in which Chogha Zanbil's Elam was an equal player.

6.matchText: Hittite king Mursili I had carried the statue off in his famous 1595 BCE sack of Babylon
6.blurb: Mursili I's Hittite raid in 1595 BCE ended the Old Babylonian dynasty and was the first documented kidnapping of the Marduk statue from Babylon. Mesopotamia Chapter 8 tells the story of the raid that opened the Kassite period — Shutruk-Nahhunte's 1158 BCE sack was the third iteration of a kidnapping game the Babylonians had been playing for 437 years.

6.matchText: Assyrian king Tukulti-Ninurta I had done it again around 1225 BCE
6.blurb: Tukulti-Ninurta I briefly conquered Babylon around 1225 BCE and carried off the Marduk statue to Assyria — the second of three kidnappings before Shutruk-Nahhunte's 1158 BCE sack. Mesopotamia Chapter 9 covers Assyria's first rise and the statue's Assyrian interlude.

Nebuchadnezzar I — the first king of that name, not to be confused with the Neo-Babylonian Nebuchadnezzar II six centuries later — organized the Babylonian counterattack that sacked Susa and recovered the Marduk statue. Mesopotamia Chapter 9 covers his war and its cultural aftermath, including the Enuma Elish that was composed to celebrate Marduk's return.

7.matchText: Neo-Assyrian Empire of the seventh century BCE was the first military-industrial complex in world history
7.blurb: The Neo-Assyrian Empire at its peak under Tiglath-Pileser III, Sennacherib, and Ashurbanipal ran the first professional standing army, the first industrial siege-warfare capacity, and the first systematic use of forced population transfer as a strategic tool. Mesopotamia Chapter 11 tells the Assyrian side of the machine that ground Elam down.

Ashurbanipal's library at Nineveh held over 30,000 cuneiform tablets of Mesopotamian literature, religion, medicine, and history — most of what we know about Sumerian and Akkadian civilization has come down to us through it. Mesopotamia Chapter 11 covers the scholar-king side of the same man who hung Te-Umman's head in his garden.

8.blurb: The Indo-Aryans and the Persians were the eastern and western halves of the same Bronze Age population movement — Indo-European-speaking pastoralists splitting around the Hindu Kush in the second millennium BCE. Indus Valley Chapter 10 tells the eastern half of the story: the Indo-Aryans entering the subcontinent, composing the Rigveda, and eventually becoming the cultural ancestors of Vedic India.

Cyrus took Babylon without a battle in 539 BCE, ended three thousand years of Mesopotamian political independence, and issued the Cyrus Cylinder celebrating his conquest. Mesopotamia Chapter 13 covers the fall of Babylon and the long echo of Mesopotamian civilization under Persian, Greek, Parthian, and Sasanian rule.
